question: What is an 'autonomous routine' in robotics programming?

options.A: A remote control mode where a human steers the robot using a joystick
options.B: A list of pre-programmed steps a robot follows completely by itself without human help
options.C: An error message that pops up when a motor gets disconnected
options.D: A game mode where robots compete to see who can type Python faster

question:
Look at this autonomous routine method:

def perform_task_mix_and_match(self):
    self.move_forward(50)
    self.turn(90)
    self.grab()

In what order will the robot execute these actions?

options.A: It will grab first, then turn, then move forward
options.B: It will do all three actions at the exact same split second
options.C: It will execute them one by one in order, from top to bottom
options.D: It will pick a random step to do first

question:
Look at this method definition from the blueprint:

def turn(self, angle):
    print(f"Turning {angle} degrees")

If you want your routine code to command the robot to turn 45 degrees, how should you call it?
If you are writing an autonomous routine inside a class method, why do you type 'self.' before calling other methods (like 'self.grab()')?

options.A: To inform the computer that the program is finished running
options.B: To tell Python to run a method that belongs to *this specific robot object*
options.C: To create a brand new infinite loop
options.D: To delete the previous movement instruction

question: What is the ultimate goal of combining classes, attributes, and methods together into an autonomous routine?

options.A: To make the python notebook change background colors
options.B: To organize complex robot behaviors into structured, repeatable steps so the robot can complete full missions reliably
options.C: To prevent the robot's battery level from dropping below 50 percent
options.D: To hide your code secrets from other competition teams
